Title: Risk of Jaslinda attacked by wild animals, fall is low, says mountain guide
Article:
TAPAH: The risk of 49-year-old Jaslinda Saludin having been attacked by wild animals or suffering a fall is low, according to the mountain guide leading the search for the missing hiker at Gunung Batu Putih.
Jaslinda was last seen by a mountain guide about 7.30am on Sunday, May 24.
Mountain guide Muzafar Mohamad said the likelihood of either incident was small, based on Jaslinda's previous experience climbing the mountain.
"At this point, mental and physical endurance also play an important role.
"So I hope she is able to get through this critical test after more than a week of search operations.
"In terms of survival skills, Jaslinda's more than 10 years of experience in mountaineering may give her an advantage," he said when met at the Fire and Rescue Department's command centre for the search and rescue (SAR) mission at Surau At-Taqwa, Sultan Idris II Power Station, Kuala Woh, near here.
The SAR operation for the missing hiker
intensified yesterday
with 16 additional rescuers.
The Perak Fire and Rescue Department said the additional rescuers comprise six members of the department's Special Tactical Operation and Rescue Team (Storm), two Department of Wildlife and National Parks (Perhilitan) protection team members, five Forestry Department personnel, and three forestry mountain guides.
Jaslinda was reported to have been among a group of 13 people and two mountain guides who began the Trans Spencer Chapman climb about 2am on May 23, taking the route through Pos Gedung-Gunung Bah Gading-Gunung Batu Putih-Kuala Woh.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Deaf' remark not aimed at Pas, Tun Faisal tells Ahmad Fadhli
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A Bersatu leader has questioned why Pas information chief Ahmad Fadhli Shaari reacted defensively to a "deaf" label used during a Bersatu ceramah in Melaka recently.
Bersatu information chief Datuk Tun Faisal Ismail Aziz said he believed the remarks made by party secretary-general Datuk Seri Azmin Ali, who used the term "deaf" to describe those claiming the party lacks election machinery, were not directed at Pas.
Tun Faisal said Azmin's remark was aimed at specific individuals and should not have caused offence to those uninvolved.
"I do not think it was directed at Pas, but perhaps at certain individuals who deliberately refuse to listen to explanations provided by Bersatu leaders.
"It is also for individuals who failed to convey accurate information regarding decisions made during the Perikatan Nasional supreme council meetings.
"The same applies to individuals who slandered Bersatu with heavy accusations, using disrespectful words like 'get out' and claiming we were untrustworthy in fulfilling promises... but it is not about Pas," he said in a statement.
Tun Faisal was responding to Ahmad Fadhli, after the latter suggested it was time for Pas to move independently of Bersatu in future elections.
Ahmad Fadhli's remarks were triggered by a viral video of Azmin's speech in Melaka, where he described the claim that Bersatu
lacks machinery
as a "deaf" perspective.
It was previously reported that Pas president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang had claimed that Bersatu only demanded to
field candidates
in by-elections and state elections but lacked sufficient machinery to carry out effective campaigning.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man with 24 priors arrested over Cheras kopitiam gold chain-snatch theft
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have arrested a man linked to a gold chain-snatch theft in Taman Connaught last month.
A gold chain and pendant estimated to be worth RM14,000 were snatched from a man who was having breakfast with his wife at a kopitiam in Taman Connaught on April 12.
Cheras police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Rosdi Daud said the couple were seated outdoors when a man on a motorcycle approached from behind.
The suspect then fled with the jewellery.
"Police conducted a raid at 2.30pm on May 24 in Bandar Kinrara, Puchong, leading to the arrest of a man in his mid-30s in the vicinity of Jalan Klang Lama.
"The suspect has a criminal and drug record of 24 prior offences," he said in a statement today.
The case is being investigated under Section 392 of the Penal Code for robbery.
Mohd Rosdi said the court rejected the police application to remand the suspect, who has since been released on police bail.
"The investigation paper has been completed and will be referred to the Attorney-General's Chambers," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Optimax sees strong volume recovery despite higher costs, says HLIB
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Optimax Holdings Bhd has not experienced any significant increase in supply-related costs, including consumables, despite the ongoing Iran war at this juncture.
According to Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB), the company has seen a strong rebound in business volume during the April–May period, with the momentum expected to continue into the second quarter of financial year 2026 (Q2 FY26).
It added that revenue could potentially track close to the record level achieved in Q4 FY25.
HLIB also expects Optimax to incur marginally higher expenses following staff hiring in June 2026 ahead of the commencement of its 70 per cent-owned facility in Indonesia, which is targeted to begin operations between the third and fourth quarters of financial year 2026 (FY26).
According to HLIB, the company estimated the additional cost at between RM20,000 and RM30,000 per month but said it is expected to be offset by stronger revenue growth.
HLIB noted that Optimax posted a core profit after tax and minority interest (PATMI) of RM2.8 million in Q1 FY26, down 30.6 per cent quarter-on-quarter and 17.6 per cent year-on-year (YoY), which it deemed in line with both its own and consensus full-year forecasts at 16 per cent and 17 per cent, respectively.
It said the first quarter was seasonally softer due to festive disruptions from Chinese New Year, Ramadan and Hari Raya.
However, the firm noted that business volume rebounded strongly in April and May, with revenue potentially nearing the record level seen in Q4 FY25.
Following the results, HLIB maintained its FY26 and FY27 forecasts while introducing its FY28 earnings projection of RM21.3 million, representing a 12 per cent YoY growth.
The firm also reiterated its "Buy" call on Optimax, although it lowered its target price to 72 sen from 85 sen.
HLIB said the lower valuation multiple reflects the absence of meaningful positive earnings surprises since its initiation coverage, despite earlier expectations for stronger upside delivery.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Search for missing hiker Jaslinda intensifies with additional manpower
Article:
IPOH: The search and rescue operation for missing female hiker Jaslinda Saludin, 49, at Gunung Batu Putih near Tapah is now being intensified with additional manpower.
Perak Fire and Rescue Department director Datuk Sayani Saidon said the additional personnel are part of a plan to mount an integrated, thorough operation in a focused search sector.
As of 10am, 21 rescue personnel remain in the Gunung Batu Putih search sector, 13 from the department, three from the Forestry Department, and five Perak Forestry Mountain Guides (MGP).
"Communication with the rescue team is still down, believed to be due to them being in a valley with no coverage," she said in a statement today.
She added that at 1.13am, 17 Civil Defence Force members exited through Pos Musoh, while five others — one fireman and four Forestry staff — exited through Pos Kuala Woh at 2.12am.
Sayani said six firemen have reached the peak of Gunung Rayu. They are in good condition, have sufficient food and water supplies, and have had adequate rest.
"However, the team on the ground is facing communication constraints because the operation area has no phone coverage, preventing them from sending direct reports to duty officers," she said.
She said information received from the SAR team at 12.30pm confirmed that a wildlife camera trap belonging to the Department of Wildlife and National Parks (Perhilitan) in the Bukit Berapit 2 area had been checked, but no images of the victim were found.
She added that the SAR team, together with Perhilitan personnel, then moved to the camera location at Gunung Rayu to retrieve another memory card for analysis.
"The Gunung Rayu camera is located approximately one kilometre off the Sukaneka–Gunung Rayu–Bukit Berapit–Pos Musoh trekking route. Nevertheless, the inspection was carried out to determine whether the victim may have strayed from the main route and passed by that camera," she explained.
"For the record, based on the camera's coordinates, that area falls under Hulu Jelai, Lipis, Pahang. The SAR operation continues around Gunung Rayu with department and MGP personnel," said Sayani.
Previously, Jaslinda was reported to have begun a Trans Spencer Chapman climb with 13 other individuals and two MGP at 2 am on May 23, via the Pos Gedung–Gunung Bah Gading–Gunung Batu Putih–Kuala Woh route.
Jaslinda and another climber, Mohd Hanafi Neikmad, 41, experienced health issues and stopped. However, Jaslinda was reported to have continued her journey toward the mountain peak.
She was last seen by an MGP on May 24 at 7.30am.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MBM Resources to benefit from strong Perodua demand, policy support
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: MBM Resources Bhd is poised to benefit from sustained demand for Perodua vehicles, backed by a healthy order backlog of about 43,000 units and continued strong new bookings.
Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB) said supportive government measures, including the RM4,000 matching grant for scrapping vehicles aged over 20 years for purchases of Perodua and Proton models, are expected to further lift demand.
The investment bank also noted that tax exemptions for private hire and taxi operators purchasing Perodua and Proton vehicles from 2026 are likely to provide an additional boost to vehicle sales.
HLIB said the recent launch of Perodua's new B-segment sport utility vehicle (SUV), the Traz, has received encouraging demand.
In addition, it believes recent electric vehicle (EV) policy developments will benefit completely knocked down (CKD) EV original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), noting that Perodua's Q-VE model has already garnered 1,700 orders.
HLIB noted that the group continues to face softer demand for Volvo and Volkswagen vehicles.
However, the firm said after-sales volumes have been improving, while the recent appreciation of the ringgit is expected to benefit Perodua through lower input costs.
Following updates from MBM Resources' financial year 2025 (FY25) annual report, HLIB made minor earnings revisions, lowering its FY26 forecast by 1.7 per cent and FY27 by 8.0 per cent, while introducing an FY28 earnings forecast of RM338.6 million.
The firm maintained its "Buy" call on MBM Resources with an unchanged target price of RM7.10.
HLIB said MBM Resources remains in a strong net cash position of 62.2 sen per share, underpinned by sustainable earnings and cash flow driven by Perodua's strong sales performance.
On its financial performance, MBM Resources reported a core profit after tax and minority interests (PATMI) of RM63.5 million for the first quarter of FY26.
HLIB said the results came within expectations, accounting for 19.7 per cent of its full-year forecast and 19.8 per cent of consensus estimates.
The firm expects stronger earnings in subsequent quarters, as the current quarter was affected by long holidays and plant shutdowns.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel troops capture Beaufort Castle in southern Lebanon push against Hizbollah
Article:
TEL AVIV: Israeli troops have seized the 900-year-old Beaufort Castle and its strategic ridge in southern Lebanon, the military said on Sunday, a major advance against Iran-backed Hizbollah despite a ceasefire announced more than six weeks ago.
It comes after Saturday, one of the heaviest days of Hizbollah fire toward northern Israel since the April ceasefire, prompting school closures and restrictions.
The operation, the military said, was focused on establishing control of the Beaufort Ridge and the Wadi al-Saluki area, while degrading the Hizbollah militia and its infrastructure on the ridge established under Iranian direction.
One Israeli soldier was killed, the military said.
There were no immediate comments from Lebanon or from Hizbollah.
The capture of the medieval castle and ridge deepen Israel's footprint in Lebanon as the Israel-Hizbollah military front remains active even as a parallel ceasefire holds in the wider Iran war.
Hizbollah entered the US-Israeli war against Iran by firing rockets and drones into Israel on March 2, days after the Iran conflict began. Israel then began to attempt driving the Iran-backed militia away from its northern border.
The advance into Beaufort Castle has granted Israeli troops an overlook point over much of southern Lebanon and northern Israel, from which attacks have been launched towards Israeli residential areas.
Hizbollah "carried out numerous attacks," from the Ridge, the military said, adding that its troops were operating against launch infrastructure in the area, from which "hundreds of projectiles were launched toward Israeli civilians and IDF soldiers."
Israeli troops were also operating near Nabatieh, a major Hizbollah stronghold in southern Lebanon, the military said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US castigates Europe over defence spend as Nato reassures Asia
Article:
SINGAPORE: The United States fired a fresh broadside at its Nato allies in Singapore over the weekend but Western European officials insisted the grouping remains resilient.
Speaking at the Shangri-La Dialogue, US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth praised Asian partners for boosting defence spending and aligning closely with Washington as tensions with China mount.
"When our interests align, we act together with focused resolve," he said.
"When our interests diverge, we adjust pragmatically without the drama or the moralising. I think Western Europe might take note."
"Europe and Nato have some big decisions to make," he said.
President Donald Trump's administration has repeatedly accused European governments of underinvesting in their militaries and relying too heavily on US protection, while urging both Europe and Asian allies to boost defence spending to 3.5 per cent of GDP.
Washington announced plans in May to pull 5,000 troops out of Germany, and Trump has threatened to pull out of Nato.
A senior Nato official sought to downplay the US troop withdrawal, saying it was already planned and that the alliance's cohesion was unaffected.
"In a mature alliance, if one ally which in this case is the principal stakeholder needs to redirect some power somewhere else, he can do so, and the others must be able to step in," said Admiral Giuseppe Cavo Dragone, chair of the Nato Military Committee.
Nils Hilmer, state secretary at Germany's Federal Ministry of Defence, said Berlin was accelerating military investment regardless of future US deployments.
"What we know for sure... is that there's going to be shifts in that field," he said. "That's why we are about to take the security in our own hands."
European ministers also used the forum to reassure Asian partners that Nato remained credible beyond its immediate neighbourhood.
"Our credibility in Asia also depends on our robustness in Europe, defending Ukraine in the face of the Russian aggression," said French Defence Minister Catherine Vautrin in a speech to delegates.
Other European defence ministers argued that security theatres are increasingly intertwined.
"The European Atlantic and the Indo-Pacific theatres are becoming inseparable," said Tore Sandvik, Norway's minister of defence, noting that North Korean troops were fighting in Ukraine.
"The US will be occupied in more theatres," he said.
But for all the criticism from the Pentagon, several US Senators and members of the House of Representatives said they were seeking to reassure European and Asian allies that they had bipartisan support from Congress.
"I've heard the same anxiety from everyone, not just in the region," said US Senator Tammy Duckworth.
"I've actually got Nato allies worried about America's commitment to the Indo-Pacific."
Still, scepticism among other delegates lingers over Europe's pace of change on investing in their collective security.
"Europe has to learn how to become a player," said Pavlo Klimkin, non-resident senior fellow at the Carnegie Endowment and a former Ukrainian minister of foreign affairs.
"There is no way around it. But it could be extremely beneficial for their partnership with the United States, because the States would respect such European drive."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Redefining our economic resilience in the face of Middle East crisis
Article:
The crisis in the Middle East is a direct hit to Malaysia's supply lines, fiscal space, and industrial core. We often frame these conflicts through the narrow lens of "oil price hikes". But that is an incomplete diagnosis. This was reiterated at a recent forum hosted by UKM Minda, in collaboration with UAC and MIER. We were told that 70 per cent of our consumption—our exports, our manufacturing, and our tourism—lives or dies by the stability of global supply chains and foreign demand.
The disruption is not only about higher petrol prices. Take shipping, for example. The closure—or even the threat of closure—of the Strait of Hormuz triggers an immediate "force majeure" from global insurers. No insurance, no movement. Consequently, charter rates haven't just risen; they have more than tripled. For a trading nation like Malaysia, this is a tax on every physical good we buy and sell.
The impact is eerily specific. Chemical-based industries—from plastics to pharmaceuticals—are gasping. Many active pharmaceutical ingredients and industrial precursors transit through that corridor. A shortage of propylene glycol or polyethylene doesn't just hurt factories; it delays medicine production and raises the cost of everything from car bumpers to IV bags. Take construction small- and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), for instance. It is bleeding cash. Machinery runs on diesel, but diesel subsidy rationalisation—while fiscally necessary—has left many SMEs exposed. Not every logistics firm or contractor enjoys the same blanket protection as other groups. When diesel costs spike and loan repayments remain fixed, a mid-sized contractor can go from solvent to insolvent in three months.
We need pre-emptive, not reactive, measures. Banks should automatically trigger a six-month repayment moratorium for construction and transport SMEs during a declared supply shock, not wait for a crisis of non-performing loans. Then there is hoarding. Rising oil prices breed panic. Panic breeds hoarding of raw materials. This creates artificial scarcity, driving prices even higher. Malaysia's domestic trade authorities must move faster on anti-hoarding enforcement before the public feels the pinch.
While energy-importing nations panic, Malaysia is also an energy exporter. The spike in oil and gas revenue gives Putrajaya temporary fiscal space. The opportunity now is to funnel that windfall into energy transition capital—specifically, into renewable energy infrastructure and energy storage. Use the crisis of fossil fuel supply to accelerate the reduction of our own industrial dependence on diesel and natural gas.
Tourist arrivals from Europe and China are dropping because those economies are slowing. But Middle Eastern outbound tourism—from the Gulf states themselves—is often cash-rich and looking for safe, halal-friendly, non-Western destinations. Malaysia has not aggressively marketed itself as a premium "conflict-free getaway" for the Gulf region. That is a missed opportunity.
History tells us that there will always be crisis. Malaysia's mistake would be to treat this as a one-off oil shock. It is not. It is a structural realignment of global trade routes. The Red Sea, the Strait of Hormuz, the Strait of Malacca—these are no longer just shipping lanes; they are geopolitical fault lines.
Finally, let's address the most dangerous assumption: that because domestic spending is only 30 per cent of gross domestic product (GDP), we should focus only on exports. That 30 per cent is the social floor. When imported inflation from shipping and oil raises the cost of food, medicine, and construction materials, that 30 per cent of the economy—the part that employs millions in retail, food and beverage (F&B), and services—gets crushed. Meanwhile, the export sector faces slowing global demand. We are caught in a scissors movement: costs are rising from supply shocks, and revenues are falling from external slowdowns. That is why pre-emptive measures are not optional; they are existential.
A way forward may include creating a strategic diesel reserve for SMEs: Also, a ring-fenced, subsidised diesel allocation specifically for construction and logistics SMEs, outside the general subsidy framework. Good to trigger automatic six-month principal moratoriums for affected sectors when global freight indices cross a certain threshold. Use our port infrastructure to offer tax incentives for companies to store critical raw materials (plastic precursors, medicines) on Malaysian soil, turning us from a transit hub into a resilience hub. Direct tourism promotions to Gulf states, offering premium halal ecotourism and medical travel packages.
The world will not become stable again. That is the new normal. Malaysia can either react to each crisis with patchwork subsidies, or we can read the writing on the hull of every uninsured tanker: Supply chains are now weapons. And we need an economy that can fight back. The blessing, if we choose to see it, is that this crisis has finally killed the illusion of cheap, predictable globalisation. For a nation like Malaysia, that is not a threat. That is an invitation to build something more resilient.
--------------------------------
*The author is affiliated with the Tan Sri Omar Centre for STI Policy Studies at UCSI University and is an adjunct professor at the Ungku Aziz Centre for Development Studies, Universiti Malaya.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 52 cases, 114 people: Missing hiker cases up nearly 30pct this year
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: There have been 52 recorded cases of hikers getting lost this year, involving 114 people.
Malaysian Fire and Rescue Department director-general Datuk Seri Nor Hisham Mohammad said it is an increase of nearly 30 per cent compared with 40 cases involving 88 victims during the same period last year.
He said among the major cases this year were 10 people who got lost at Bukit Kiara Federal Park on May 9, as well as seven hikers in the Kem Kasut area of Gunung Yong Belar, Gua Musang, in February.
"I believe this increase in cases is closely related to the public's growing interest in hiking and outdoor recreational activities, especially in hilly and forested areas that are easily accessible near cities, which has also attracted new and inexperienced participants.
"The public should not take safety lightly. Physical preparation and basic knowledge are very important to prevent unwanted incidents, including getting lost in the forest.
"Last year, a total of 88 missing cases involving 193 victims were reported, with Selangor recording the highest number of search and rescue (SAR) operations for lost hikers," he said.
Nor Hisham said areas such as Bukit Kiara, Bukit Gasing and Bukit Sungai Besi recorded a high number of cases.
In Penang, he said cases are reported every year in locations such as Cherok Tok Kun, Bukit Mertajam, Bukit Jambul, Batu Ferringhi, Balik Pulau and Teluk Bahang.
"Selangor remains the state with the highest number of SAR operations for missing hikers, with cases concentrated in popular hiking spots around the Klang Valley," he said.
He said hiking activities are also exposed to uncontrollable risks such as extreme weather, unclear trails, steep terrain and other external factors.
The latest tragedy happened last week, involving two separate incidents at two locations.
At Gunung Batu Putih in Tapah, Perak, two participants of the extreme Trans Spencer Chapman expedition — Mohd Hanafi Neikmad, 41 and Jaslinda Saludin, 49 separated from the main group.
Mohd Hanafi suffered a medical emergency, experiencing shortness of breath and vomiting blood at Kem Batu Kor before being evacuated by helicopter from the Fire and Rescue Department air unit.
Jaslinda, meanwhile, chose to continue the ascent alone towards the summit at 7.30am on May 24, and that was the last time she was seen.
As of now, the SAR operation, which entered its seventh day yesterday, is being intensified using heat-detecting drones and assistance from Orang Asli trackers.
A water bottle and other belongings believed to be Jaslinda's were found along the Ayer Busok River route.
On the same day, Nur Izzati Humaira Azizul, 19, also became a victim at Bukit Changkat Asa, Hulu Selangor.
Her body was found on May 26 in an orchard, about 500m from her last known location, fully clothed and intact.
Police classified the case as sudden death, believed to be due to extreme exhaustion or heatstroke, with no criminal elements involved.
Recorded cases since 2022
Hiker Tee Kok Soon
, 55, was reported unconscious after trekking about 300m from the base of Gunung Pulai in Kulai, Johor, on Oct 30, 2022.
He was brought down in a four-wheel-drive vehicle but was confirmed dead at the foothills by medical personnel.
On Jan 23, 2023,
Haniff Harun
, 47, fell and was swept away by strong currents while crossing Sungai Tanum near Gunung Tahan in Lipis due to bad weather.
His body was later found drowned and wedged between rocks about 300m from where he initially fell.
Steven Tan Kim Huat
, 45, was reported missing on March 14, 2023, after hiking Bukit Beruang in Melaka alone at 4.30pm.
After the official SAR operation was called off, his body was eventually found by a paranormal practitioner on March 18, 2023, at 10.25am in a small stream near an abandoned trail and was confirmed dead.
On May 19, 2023,
Awang Askandar Ampuan Yaacub
, 56, was confirmed dead at 10.30am Nepal time while receiving treatment at Camp 4 of Mount Everest after a fall.
On the same expedition, Muhammad Hawari Hashim, 33, was reported missing on May 20, 2023, after reaching the summit.
The expedition team is currently in the process of applying for an official declaration of death from the government of Nepal.
Adi Putra Affendi
, 16, was reported missing on May 28, 2024, after being separated from his group at Bukit Broga, Semenyih.
After a 19-hour SAR operation, his body was found in a 10m ravine.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Explainer: Inside Malaysia's B15 move
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Effective June 1, Malaysia will advance its energy transition by raising the Peninsular biodiesel blend from B10 to B15, a move aimed at easing dependence on imported diesel while giving a bigger role to palm oil in the country's fuel mix.
At its core, the policy means every litre of diesel will now contain 15 per cent palm-based biodiesel (palm methyl ester), up from 10 per cent previously. The rest remains conventional petroleum diesel. The change may appear technical but the impact is felt more in the backdrop of the economy, especially in plantations, refineries and fuel supply chains.
The B15 biodiesel will be produced by 19 licensed plants in an effort to lower diesel prices nationwide.
For Malaysia's palm oil industry, the shift translates into a meaningful lift in domestic demand. Annual usage for biodiesel is expected to rise to about 801,000 tonnes, compared with 534,000 tonnes under the B10 programme.
That basicially means that the additional uptake will provide a steady local market for crude palm oil (CPO) and support smallholders, even as overall supply remains comfortable at a surplus estimated at around four million tonnes.
Officials say the rollout will be phased to avoid price distortions in crude palm oil and to ensure producers can adjust smoothly. Importantly, the higher domestic usage is not expected to affect Malaysia's export flows, which stand at roughly 16 million tonnes a year.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi, who is also in charge of the Rural and Regional Development Ministry, has said that raising the blend from B10 to B15 or even B20 could save Malaysia about RM1.5 billion annually in fuel subsidies.
He said that production would be introduced in stages, beginning with B15 before progressing to B20, with the potential of reaching B50 within the next two to three years.
This phased approach is intended to ensure that CPO prices do not put pressure on biodiesel production costs.
Who stands to gain?
The biggest beneficiaries from this move are likely to be along the palm oil value chain, from plantation owners and refiners to smallholders who depend on stable demand for fresh fruit bunches. Rural development agencies are also expected to benefit as more palm oil is absorbed into higher-value downstream uses.
For motorists and transport operators, the change is largely invisible. Malaysia has already tested higher blends such as B20 in parts of Langkawi, Labuan and Sarawak without major engine issues, and officials say modern diesel engines are fully compatible with B15.
Energy analysts say the policy could also provide a buffer against global shocks. By relying more on domestic biofuel, Malaysia can stretch diesel stockpiles further and reduce exposure to supply disruptions, especially during periods of geopolitical tension.
The move also fits Malaysia's climate ambitions beyond energy security. Biodiesel use helps cut greenhouse gas emissions and supports the country's longer-term shift toward lower-carbon fuels.
For now, the government is betting on a careful, gradual transition — one that quietly reshapes the fuel mix without disrupting the vehicles, industries and consumers that depend on it every day.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bayan Lepas road rage: Drug-positive Myvi driver's remand extended
Article:
BALIK PULAU: Police have obtained a two-day remand extension for a 37-year-old man following a road rage incident in which he allegedly criminally intimidated another driver and made obscene gestures along Jalan Tun Dr Awang on Thursday.
Southwest district police chief Assistant Commissioner Anuwal Ab Wahab said the remand extension began today.
"We have managed to obtain a two-day remand extension against the man to facilitate further investigation into the case.
"He is expected to be charged on Wednesday," he told the New Straits Times when contacted.
In the 11.27am incident,
the suspect, who was driving a Perodua Myvi, allegedly cut abruptly in front of the complainant's vehicle before stopping.
He then stepped out of his car, wielded an object believed to be a metal rod and swung it towards the complainant's vehicle.
The incident was captured on the complainant's dashboard camera and the footage later went viral on social media, drawing public attention.
Police subsequently arrested the suspect following investigations into the incident.
Checks revealed that he had previous criminal and drug-related records, while a preliminary urine screening test returned positive for methamphetamine.
The case is being investigated under Section 506 of the Penal Code for criminal intimidation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: China patrols Scarborough Shoal after Philippines warns of threat
Article:
BEIJING:  China's military and coast guard said they carried out patrols near disputed waters in the South China Sea on Sunday, a day after the Philippines said it remained under threat from Beijing despite a recent easing in US-China tensions.
Philippine and US forces held a five-day maritime exercise in the same waters last week near the Scarborough Shoal, the third such drill this year, to strengthen interoperability and maritime security, the Philippine military said on Sunday.
Scarborough Shoal, in waters that Manila calls the West Philippine Sea, is one of Asia's most contested maritime features and has become a frequent flashpoint between China and the Philippines over sovereignty and fishing rights.
CHINA CLAIMS 'RIGHTS VIOLATIONS, PROVOCATIVE ACTS'
The People's Liberation Army's Southern Theatre Command said in a statement on the WeChat platform that its naval and air units carried out combat readiness patrols in the "territorial sea and airspace" of the atoll and its surrounding areas.
"Such patrols serve as an effective countermeasure to cope with all sorts of rights violations and provocative acts", the command said. It did not mention specific countries.
China's coast guard said in a separate statement it conducted law enforcement patrols near the Scarborough Shoal, adding that since this month it had dealt with ships engaged in "illegal rights-violation activities in accordance with laws and regulations", without elaborating.
The Armed Forces of the Philippines said the exercise with the US from Tuesday to Saturday, including visit-board-search-and-seizure drills, underscored both countries' commitment to stronger defence ties, improved maritime domain awareness and support for a rules-based order at sea.
The Philippine embassy in Beijing did not immediately respond to an emailed request for comment.
'SEVERE THREAT'
China's patrols took place as defence ministers, military chiefs and policymakers from the Asia-Pacific region and beyond gathered in Singapore for the Shangri-La Dialogue, Asia's premier defence forum.
Speaking to Reuters on the sidelines of the meeting, Philippine Defence Secretary Gilberto Teodoro said Manila remains under "severe threat" from China territorially and politically, despite a recent thaw in US-China tensions following a summit between Presidents Donald Trump and Xi Jinping this month.
"We have no choice but really to be resilient and to stand up against Chinese aggression," he said.
The Philippines and China have been locked in repeated maritime standoffs in the South China Sea in recent years, at times resulting in collisions between vessels and injuries to personnel.
China claims sovereignty over almost the entire South China Sea via a "nine-dash line" on its maps that cuts into the exclusive economic zones of the Philippines, Brunei, Malaysia, Vietnam and Indonesia.
In ⁠2016, the Permanent Court of Arbitration in The Hague ruled that China's claims were not supported by international ⁠law, ​a decision that Beijing rejects.
Taiwan makes broadly similar claims to China's in the South China Sea.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MR DIY seen weathering Middle East tensions, conrtinues expansion
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The impact of escalating tensions in the Middle East on MR DIY Group (M) Bhd has remained manageable so far, supported by the group's strong balance sheet, bargaining power and a firmer ringgit, according to RHB Research.
RHB Research analyst Soong Wei Siang said the retailer has been able to optimise unit costs despite global cost pressures, leveraging its scale and procurement strength amid geopolitical uncertainties.
He added that the strengthening ringgit has further cushioned imported cost increases, enabling MR DIY to roll out its "Prices Stay the Same" campaign – a price guarantee initiative aimed at attracting increasingly value-conscious consumers amid inflationary pressures.
Soong also noted that MR DIY is expanding its participation in the government's Sumbangan Asas Rahmah (SARA) programme, with the number of participating stores expected to rise to about 250 by the first half of 2026. The move is anticipated to boost customer traffic across its retail network.
At the same time, MR DIY is accelerating its expansion in Sabah and Sarawak, which are emerging as key growth markets for the group.
According to Soong, about 15 per cent of the company's new outlets are now being opened in East Malaysia, up from approximately 11 to 12 per cent in previous years.
He said the increased focus on Sabah and Sarawak should support earnings growth, given the stronger profitability profile of stores in East Malaysia due to a less competitive operating environment.
"This would bode well considering the superior profitability of East Malaysia stores, thanks to the less intense competitive landscape," he said in a note.
On external risks, Soong said MR DIY has so far experienced a manageable impact from ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, particularly in terms of cost pressures.
He noted that the company is leveraging its balance sheet strength and bargaining power to optimise unit costs, further aided by the strong ringgit.
"With that, Mr DIY has launched a price lock campaign, which should appeal to value-conscious consumers in the inflationary environment," he said.
Soong said MR DIY's first-quarter results for 2026 came in broadly within expectations, supported by gross profit margin tailwinds, positive same-store sales growth and continued nationwide outlet expansion.
MR DIY posted a net profit of RM192 million for the quarter, representing 27 per cent of RHB Research's full-year forecast and 28 per cent of consensus estimates.
Group revenue increased nine per cent to RM1.4 billion, driven by the net addition of 123 new stores, bringing its total branch count to 1,583 stores.
Meanwhile, same-store sales growth remained positive at 1.6 per cent, marking a second consecutive quarter of expansion and signalling resilient consumer demand.
MR DIY also declared a first interim dividend of 1.6 sen per share, higher than the 1.4 sen announced in the corresponding quarter last year.
Following the results, RHB Research maintained its earnings forecasts and reiterated a "Buy" recommendation on the stock with an unchanged target price of RM2.20.
Soong believes the company's share price rally year-to-date may still have room to run, as MR DIY is well-positioned to benefit from consumer downtrading amid rising energy costs, supported by its ability to maintain competitive pricing.
He also said the company's improving return on equity, driven by a high dividend payout, could help narrow its valuation gap against other large-cap peers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tuan Ibrahim denies failing to pay service centre rent
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Pas deputy president Datuk Seri Tuan Ibrahim Tuan Man has dismissed allegations suggesting that he failed to pay rent for his Kubang Kerian constituency service centre in Kelantan.
The Kubang Kerian member of Parliament also said he has instructed his lawyer to take action against those spreading the slander, in order to clear both his name and that of Pas.
Tuan Ibrahim said he had diligently settled his office's monthly rent from January last year until April this year.
However, the rental payment for May was deducted from his security deposit following an eviction notice that allegedly violated the tenancy agreement.
"I have, without fail, settled the monthly rental payments.
"The rental payment for May was deducted from the excess deposit currently held, as advised by legal counsel, following an eviction instruction from the landlord that did not comply with our tenancy agreement," he said in a statement today.
He urged those responsible for spreading the false claims to cease the slander immediately.
Tuan Ibrahim lamented that despite some parties issuing apologies, the damage had already been done as the false allegations continued to circulate online.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Melaka hotel apologises, offers full refunds after system glitch triggers check-in chaos
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A hotel in Melaka has apologised to guests affected by a severe check-in disruption over the weekend.
In a statement posted on its Facebook page, Birkin International Hotel said it had offered refunds and compensation to all affected guests.
The hotel also said it has taken additional measures to strengthen its booking controls and operational processes to prevent a recurrence.
"We acknowledge the challenges experienced during the recent check-in process and sincerely apologise to those affected. Due to an unexpected system glitch that led to overbooking and operational challenges, room preparation took longer than anticipated, resulting in delayed check-ins for some guests," the statement said.
Hotel finance director Jac Khoo told the New Straits Times that the operational chaos was triggered by a technical failure within a third-party and wholesale booking system, rather than the hotel's internal system.
The glitch allegedly allowed a massive and uncontrolled influx of last-minute bookings and walk-in reservations to continue being processed until 10pm, overwhelming the property's available room inventory.
"The situation was brought under control by midnight on Sunday. After discussions with guests, we provided rooms that had not been fully prepared so they could rest while our team completed the remaining housekeeping work.
"Our general manager met with all affected guests and explained the full refund and compensation package, which they agreed to. We will begin processing the refunds on Wednesday, after banking operations resume following the long weekend holiday," Khoo said.
She added that the hotel also provided complimentary breakfast and dinner to guests affected by the disruption.
Khoo confirmed that guests who chose to cancel their stays and return home would still be eligible for a full refund and compensation package.
The apology comes after widespread outrage on social media, with affected guests expressing frustration over lengthy waiting times and confusion during the check-in process.
One affected guest
shared their experience online
, claiming they had not received a room key despite waiting for more than five hours.
They alleged that some hotel patrons were forced to purchase their own toiletries and toilet paper after being informed that housekeeping had run out of supplies.
They raised concerns over security, alleging that the hotel's lifts did not require key card access to reach guest room floors, potentially allowing unrestricted access to accommodation areas.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Europe's green jet fuels see upside in Iran war
Article:
BRUSSELS: At a plant near Frankfurt, in Germany, hydrogen and CO2 sourced from a chlorine factory and a biogas facility nearby are piped into a reactor and -- after a few more steps -- turned into jet fuel.
Interest in such synthetic propellants is growing as the Iran war pushes Europe to reassess its dependencies, raising hopes of a turnaround for the struggling sector, according to industry experts.
The conflict "made the business case for e-SAF much stronger," Mariano Berkenwald, head of strategy at Ineratec, the firm operating the Frankfurt facility, told AFP.
Before the war squeezed Europe's jet fuel supplies, electro-Sustainable Aviation Fuel (e-SAF), was largely touted for its climate friendly credentials.
Doing away with oil, it can reduce by 90 per cent planet warming emissions from aviation -- itself responsible for up to four per cent of all greenhouse gases released in the European Union -- proponents say.
Brussels has made it a key component of a push to green up transport, mandating fuel suppliers to blend at least 1.2 per cent of the stuff into the kerosene made available at EU airports by 2030 and 35 per cent by 2050.
But the sector has struggled to take off, bogged down by high costs and low investments.
'Zero' to hero?
Founded 10 years ago, Ineratec launched production last year -- and its small German facility is currently the only one in Europe making the propellant.
About 40 more projects under development are stuck in a rut, unable to secure the funding needed to start production plants, said Camille Mutrelle of advocacy group Transport & Environment (T&E).
Europe needs roughly nine more bigger factories to meet the 2030 target but "we still have zero", she said.
Ineratec's plant churns out some 2,500 tonnes of fuel a year, enough to power only about 50 transatlantic flights.
With the clock ticking, Brussels, which recently scaled down the ambition of other climate goals, notably for cars, has been under pressure to scrap the target or at least the hefty fines suppliers would face for not meeting it.
The industry was braced for a review of the mandate due next year.
It now believes the Iran war and the resulting closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a key shipping lane for global gas and oil, might have changed the momentum.
Since its key ingredients can be sourced entirely in Europe, e-SAF has drawn attention from military forces, which worry about the ready availability of fuel to power fighter jets, helicopters and other vehicles -- and could pay a premium for it, according to Matteo Mirolo, an aviation consultant specialising in sustainable fuels.
Defence interest
E-SAF costs about 10 times more than kerosene and even supporters concede it is unlikely to ever match the price of its fossil fuel-based rival.
Germany's armed forces recently tested Ineratec's fuel. And other governments are known to have expressed interest in building capacity, said Mutrelle.
"The current crisis is a wake-up call regarding Europe's energy independence," said Ourania Georgoutsakou, managing director of Airlines for Europe, a civil aviation lobby group.
Making sustainable fuels cheaper and more readily available should be "an urgent strategic priority", she added.
Supporters hope that getting governments behind the technology could finally persuade investors to bet on it.
Doubts over the sector and low prospective returns meant that energy majors have so far largely stayed away, preferring to invest in biofuels, said Mutrelle.
Derived from waste, these propellants are also part of the EU's climate plans.
But production often relies on waste oil and fat imports from China, which makes them less palatable from a security of supply perspective.
Fuel manufacturing group FuelsEurope did not reply to a request for comment.
The European Commission said it was considering the establishment of an EU financing mechanism for e-SAF to support development.
"We need home-grown energy, including fuels and aviation fuels," a commission spokesperson said.
Berkenwald of Ineratec said the company has been getting more calls of late but they are yet to translate into contracts.
"We are hopeful that that's where this is heading," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Too many flies,' say villagers enduring long-standing infestation
Article:
TAMPIN: Since last year, villagers of Kampung Ulu Air Kuning Selatan have been enduring a persistent fly infestation that has disrupted daily life, with many blaming a nearby chicken farm.
They said the situation has made daily life increasingly difficult, as flies invade homes in large numbers and disrupt basic routines, while also raising concerns over possible health risks.
Nazaruddin Johari, 49, claimed that the problem is believed to be caused by a chicken farm near the village.
"This problem has been going on for a long time and comes seasonally. The flies will disappear for two to three months after action is taken, before returning again in large numbers.
"There are so many of them in the bedroom, bathroom, living room and kitchen.
"It is very difficult for us to cook or carry out daily activities; even resting at home is uncomfortable," he said when contacted today.
Nazaruddin also questioned the existence of a chicken farm in the area, which was reportedly developed near a residential settlement.
"I really hope the elected representative will come down to the ground and see this fly problem for themselves.
"The poultry operators are making profits, but we are the ones dealing with the flies.
"Some villagers use fly traps, but there are simply too many. Even when we are talking, flies can enter our mouths. What is most worrying is the impact on children and babies," he said.
Another villager, Razman Abdul Rahim, claimed that the situation has become most severe since the poultry farm began operations nearby, and questioned its approval.
"The farm is located near four schools, which is also affecting pupils and students. If it was approved, then it should have proper systems and standard operating procedures in place.
"The relevant authorities or state government should not approve such farms purely for investment reasons without considering the problems faced by the people.
"There should be a proper buffer zone between the farm and residential areas. What also worries us is that there is a pig farm not far from here.
"The flies likely go to the pig farm and we fear they may then enter homes carrying germs or viruses," he said.
Razman said villagers hope the state government will review the approval and operations of the farm and, if it is found not to comply with regulations, call for it to be shut down
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 100pct redemption rate: Teachers top up own money to maximise Madani book vouchers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The 2025 Madani teachers' book voucher programme has fostered stronger reading habits among educators nationwide, a study has revealed.
Perbadanan Kota Buku chief executive officer Adibah Omar said findings from the programme's impact study revealed that 87.5 per cent of respondents agreed that the initiative had increased their interest in reading.
She said 428,027 teachers redeemed the vouchers, amounting to RM42.8 million, with a 100 per cent redemption rate and a 99.74 per cent book purchase rate.
"Some 87.5 per cent of respondents agreed that the initiative had increased their interest in reading, while 70.6 per cent cited greater reading activity as the main reason for wanting the programme to continue.
"63.2 per cent said they used the vouchers specifically to enhance their professional knowledge, with 49 per cent purchasing books related to professional development," she said when presenting findings at the Kuala Lumpur International Book Fair today.
The study was conducted in collaboration with the IDE Research Centre and involved 1,794 respondents from across the country.
Adibah said 67.1 per cent of respondents purchased more than three books and 56.7 per cent topped up their own money to maximise the value of the vouchers.
Meanwhile, Adibah said the study also recommended increasing the voucher value to between RM150 and RM200.
She said it suggested including digital resources such as e-books and academic journals and expanding participation among booksellers and publishers.
"There was also strong support for making this an annual initiative to ensure its long-term impact," she said.
Adibah described the programme as a strategic investment in educators and the nation's intellectual capital.
"The high participation rate and positive feedback clearly demonstrate that when teachers are empowered with access to quality reading materials, they are not only able to enhance their professional capabilities but also improve the quality of teaching and learning in the classroom," she said.
On May 29, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim announced that all secondary school students under the Education Ministry and public university students nationwide will receive RM100 book vouchers through the
Madani Book Voucher
programme.
Anwar said the vouchers can be used at the Kuala Lumpur International Book Fair as part of efforts to cultivate a reading culture and strengthen knowledge development among Malaysians.
He had said that despite the economic challenges faced by the government, education and books must continue to be prioritised.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indo-Pacific nations deepen defence ties as US doubts grow and China ascends
Article:
CAUGHT between China's rapid military rise and growing doubts about the US focus on a region it has long dominated, Indo-Pacific nations are racing to arm themselves, and each other.
At Asia's premier defence forum on Saturday, US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth pressed regional partners to shoulder more of the security burden.
Yet, he faced persistent concerns that US priorities may be drifting, with conflict in Iran competing for attention.
"We can ​do two things at one time," Hegseth told the Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore, a free-wheeling gathering of global defence chiefs, military and intelligence officials.
His Japanese counterpart Shinjiro Koizumi said he believed the US commitment was "unwavering", while acknowledging that some countries may still underestimate its resolve.
In interviews with Reuters on the sidelines of the annual gathering, regional defence chiefs and military officers made clear the push was on to do more with each other beyond the traditional US umbrella.
"All the defence secretaries here present are unanimous in the need to make agile and speedy upscaling of their own individual defence capabilities," Philippines' Defence Secretary Gilberto Teodoro told Reuters.
He described it as "buttressing" the US's traditional role, with Manila deepening defence ties with partners such as Japan, Australia, Canada and New Zealand.
"The commitment of the United States becomes more solid when more actors, at least in the deterrence phase, come in, because there is a common threat."
Japan is positioning itself as a hub for that broader network.
Koizumi said Tokyo aims to act as a "connecting point" for closer regional cooperation beyond China.
In April, Japan unveiled its biggest overhaul of defence export rules in decades, scrapping restrictions on overseas arms sales and opening the way for exports of warships, missiles and other weapons.
"Japan will be even more proactive in defence equipment cooperation," Koizumi said at the forum.
"Our aim is to ensure that each country has the capabilities it needs and to make them available when needed."
[[nid:1452185]]
Canada's Chief of Defence Staff General Jennie Carignan said her forces were expanding their presence in the region, co-operating with Japan and the Philippines on cybersecurity and maritime exercises while also helping Indonesian counterparts with English language training.
"There is a lot of work to do in the Indo-Pacific region. And I think this is why we are seeing probably an increase of partnership across the board," Carignan told Reuters.
New Zealand, meanwhile, is weighing closer ties and fresh hardware. Defence Minister Chris Penk confirmed Wellington is actively considering Japanese and British vessels to replace its ageing ANZAC-class frigates.
Penk dined with his colleagues from Singapore, Malaysia, Australia and Britain on the fringes of the dialogue as they mapped out growing interactions under their 54-year-old Five-Powered Defence Arrangement.
Penk, who took office in April, said there was scope for continuing the pact "at a more intense level."
"And so if we can find new ways to interact with others as well as maintaining those existing connections, then we'll look to do that at the same time," Penk said in an interview.
Although regional nations were deepening ties among themselves, Asian officials insisted that US commitment to the Indo-Pacific remains undimmed by the Middle East conflict or President Donald Trump's "America First" policy.
"Our confidence is not swayed by reason of the involvement of the United States in Iran, for example, and in other areas," said the Philippines' Teodoro.
For Australia, Defence Minister Richard Marles described ties with Washington as "absolutely fundamental to our national security."
"For both of us, the Trump administration and the Albanese government in Australia, we see ourselves as stewards of a relationship which goes well beyond us," Marles said.
* The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police seek public help to find missing 16-year-old girl in Temerloh
Article:
KUANTAN: Police are seeking public assistance to locate a 16-year-old girl who has been missing since May 14 in Temerloh.
In a statement today, acting Jerantut district police chief Deputy Superintendent Arshad Ayob said a missing person report on Ainul Maisarah Abu Bakar was lodged by her father about 10am yesterday.
"The teenager was last seen at Kampung Asli Pasu, Kuala Krau, Temerloh, wearing her school uniform," he said.
He said the girl has a dark complexion and a slim, tall build.
Members of the public with information are urged to contact the Jerantut district police headquarters' District Control Centre at 09-2662222 or visit the nearest police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Identity verification required for sponsored ads on social media
Article:
MELAKA: All advertisers using sponsored advertisements on social media platforms will be required to verify their identities in a bid to curb online fraud, Deputy Communications Minister Teo Nie Ching said.
She said the measure, which also targets digital gambling activities and the misuse of prominent individuals' identities, is among the new responsibilities under the Risk Mitigation Code (RMC).
She said the code will come into force tomorrow under the Online Safety Act 2025 (ONSA) and will apply to social media platform providers classified as licensees, namely those with more than eight million users in Malaysia.
"We have seen many sponsored posts using the images of well-known individuals to promote content such as dubious investments, financial scams and online gambling, and so far it has been difficult to take action because we do not know who actually paid for the advertisements," she said.
She said this to reporters after attending the launch of the Kota Melaka Parliamentary Elected Representative for the People (WRUR) programme for the Banda Hilir state constituency at Taman Kota Laksamana Cheng Ho today.
Teo said the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) will require every advertiser, whether an individual or a registered entity, to undergo identity verification by the platform before being allowed to publish sponsored advertisements.
She urged social media platform providers to fully cooperate in complying with the RMC as well as the Child Protection Code (CPC), which will also take effect tomorrow, to ensure the safety of internet users.
On the age-verification mechanism that will also take effect tomorrow to prevent individuals under the age of 16 from opening social media accounts, Teo said official documents such as identity cards or passports will be required for registration.
She acknowledged that some parents are still looking for the best approach to convince their children about the safety measures, but said the majority fully support the initiative.
"Parents are indeed very supportive because they understand that the internet presents many issues such as cyberbullying, sexual abuse and child grooming, all of which are concerns faced by parents," she said.
She said the government will provide a reasonable period for platform providers to fulfil these new responsibilities, but a high level of compliance is expected.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Odeon Cinema fire sparks wave of nostalgic recollections
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Long before the era of multiplexes, reclining seats and online bookings, the Odeon Cinema was a gathering place where generations of moviegoers came together for entertainment, family outings and cherished memories.
Although the building was gutted by fire yesterday, its heyday continues to live on in the memories of those who once filled its halls. Many recall a cinema experience far removed from today's modern standards, but rich in character and nostalgia.
For social media user Cloud Go, the former cinema brings back memories of a simpler time when affordability mattered more than comfort.
Go recalled purchasing third-class tickets for just 40 sen and sitting so close to the screen that moviegoers had to rest their necks against the backs of their chairs to watch comfortably.
"The conditions were basic. Children would squat on the floor, and rats could sometimes be seen running around. But despite all that, it was a nostalgic experience," the user said.
The Odeon Cinema also holds a special place in another social media user, Zara Zulkifli's, family history.
"It was an old-fashioned cinema with its own distinct smell and worn-out interior. It was nothing like the modern cineplexes we have in shopping malls today, but that was what going to the movies was like back then."
Laura Lau shared similar sentiments, recalling how her aunt would take her to the cinema for inexpensive late-night screenings during her childhood.
She also remembered occasions when renowned Hong Kong actors and actresses appeared on stage for live performances, leaving lasting impressions that remain vivid decades later.
"Those memories are truly precious. I can still remember them very clearly," she said.
For Visnu Varmen, the building's significance extended beyond its Odeon identity.
He remembered it as the home of Vennai Cinema, a popular venue for Tamil and other Indian-language films.
"It was where we watched many of our favourite movies. So many beautiful memories were made there," he said.
Among the films he remembered were those starring Vijay (C. Joseph Vijay), who has since become Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu.
While the building no longer serves its original purpose, recollections of former patrons highlight its enduring role as more than just a cinema.
For many, it was a cultural landmark where families bonded, friendships were formed and unforgettable movie-going experiences unfolded — a reminder of an era when the magic of the silver screen often mattered more than the comfort of the seat.
It was reported that a
fire broke out at the former Odeon Cinema building along Penang Road last night
, sending firefighters rushing to contain the blaze at one of the state's most recognisable heritage landmarks.
The building, which housed the Midnight Black nightclub, was engulfed in flames, with damage estimated at 70 per cent. The premises has since been closed for renovation works.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thousands join Wesak Day rites at temples nationwide
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: In a spirit of peace and gratitude, Wesak Day was celebrated across the country today, reflecting Malaysia's multi-racial and multi-religious society.
In Perak, Ipoh's Wat Sribunya Manggarama chairman Datuk Ching Thoo anticipates that more than 5,000 devotees will visit the temple.
He said the celebrations, which fall on a weekend, offer devotees a timely opportunity to attend religious ceremonies and activities organised at the temple.
"Devotees from Ipoh and Tambun are among the largest groups coming to the temple to perform religious rites and commemorate important events in the life of Buddhism's founder, Gautama Buddha," he told Bernama today.
He said Wesak Day is observed to commemorate three significant events in the life of Gautama Buddha; his birth, enlightenment and passing.
In Sarawak, around 3,000 Buddhists gathered at the Sarawak Buddhist Association temple in Kuching for a prayer ceremony, which was also attended by Deputy Premier and state Public Health, Housing and Local Government Minister Datuk Amar Dr Sim Kui Hian.
In Penang, Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow said Wesak Day is more than a religious celebration, as it also carries a universal message of compassion, wisdom, moderation and harmony in society.
Celebrations in the state were held in a simple yet lively atmosphere, with thousands of Buddhists visiting major temples to take part in various religious ceremonies.
Among the main focal points were Wat Chaiya Mangalaram in Lorong Burma, Wat Buppharam in Jalan Perak and Kek Lok Si in Air Itam, which began receiving devotees as early as 8am.
A festive atmosphere was also evident at the Penang Lee Sih Chong Soo (Lee Kongsi) building on Jalan Burma, where devotees of all ages came with their families to light lotus-shaped candles and lamps, offer flowers and take part in the bathing ceremony of the Buddha statue, one of the main highlights of Wesak Day celebrations.
In Perlis, Wesak Day celebrations in Kangar organised by the Perlis Buddhist Society began as early as 8am, with about 800 devotees attending to take part in religious ceremonies.
Perlis Buddhist Society president Dr Tan Chan Sin said attendance is expected to increase later in the evening, while hoping the celebration will continue to further enhance harmony within the community.
He said the celebrations also included the provision of vegetarian meals for visitors.
In Pahang, Pahang Buddhist Society secretary Wong Sien Biang said about 6,000 Buddhists from within and outside Kuantan are expected to attend the celebrations at Indera Mahkota 8.
"From our observations today, a majority of the visitors were tourists as many were unfamiliar to us. I'd say around 60 per cent were tourists and 40 per cent locals," he said.
Wong said among the key ceremonies held during the Wesak Day celebration were devotional chanting and the Bathing the Little Buddha ceremony.
In Kelantan, Wesak Day at Wat Machimaram, Tumpat, was celebrated in a lively atmosphere, with nearly 1,000 Buddhists and Siamese from within and outside the state in attendance.
Kelantan State Siamese Women's Association chairman KC Ngiam Eh Kuan said the celebration has continued to receive strong support every year.
"Every year, we have a lively celebration, and the Siamese community in Kelantan eagerly looks forward to Wesak Day. Tonight, a religious ceremony will be held at 8.30pm," she said.
In Selangor, thousands of devotees were present to take part in various spiritual and cultural activities at the Fo Guang Shan Dong Zen Temple in Jenjarom, Kuala Langat.
Hiu Li Wei said she was excited to participate in the bathing ceremony of the baby Buddha statue, which symbolises self-purification and commemorates the birth of the Buddha.
"This ceremony brings me peace and reminds me to cleanse my mind of impurities such as hatred and ignorance," she said.
Tam Choi Man, 40, said this year's celebration felt more meaningful, as the peaceful, flower-decorated temple offered a deep spiritual experience.
"Besides taking part in the bathing ceremony of the Buddha statue, I also spent time in meditation and listened to sutra recitations in the main hall," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Over 100,000 expected to throng Brickfields for Malaysia's largest Wesak Day celebration
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: More than 100,000 devotees, tourists and well-wishers are expected to throng the Buddhist Maha Vihara in Brickfields today as Malaysia's largest Wesak Day celebration gets under way with religious observances, charitable initiatives and a grand candlelight procession featuring 35 decorated floats.
The temple's honorary secretary, Tilak Leslie, said preparations for the annual celebration began two months ago to ensure the smooth running of the religious and community activities planned for devotees.
"We expect about 100,000 devotees, tourists and well-wishers to pass through our gates throughout the day, while an estimated 30,000 people will participate in the candlelight procession this evening," he said.
Leslie said the highlight of this year's celebration would be the Wesak candlelight procession, which will feature 35 floats representing the Malaysian, Nepali, Bangladeshi, Thai and Myanmar Buddhist communities.
"We are expecting Transport Minister Anthony Loke and Sri Lankan High Commissioner to Malaysia Darshana Perera to flag off the float procession about 6.30pm," he said at the temple.
Leslie said the temple's programme combines spiritual observances with acts of charity and community service in keeping with the teachings of the Buddha.
"Among the key activities are special prayers, a midnight meditation session, an Eight Precepts programme, blood and organ donation campaigns, a Silent Mentor initiative, sponsorship of 10,000 oil lamps and the construction of a Wesak thorana, a lighted display depicting significant events in the Buddha's life," he said.
He added that the temple is also distributing 10,000 free vegetarian food packs and drinks to visitors throughout the celebration.
The activities lined up, he added, were designed not only to commemorate the birth, enlightenment and passing of the Buddha, but also to encourage devotees to practise compassion, generosity and mindfulness.
He said 60 participants took part in the Eight Precepts programme on both Wesak Eve and Wesak Day, temporarily adopting a more disciplined lifestyle centred on renunciation, mindfulness and harmlessness.
"The precepts include abstaining from killing, stealing, false speech, intoxicants and other worldly indulgences."
Meanwhile, 42 participants joined an overnight meditation programme from midnight until 6am today.
"The meditation session seeks to emulate what the Buddha did more than 2,600 years ago when he attained enlightenment through deep meditation in the stillness of the night," Leslie said.
He said devotees could also receive blessings from monks during special blessing services held throughout the day.
To accommodate the large crowd, Leslie said the temple management had erected canopies in selected areas within its premises and enlisted the assistance of the People's Volunteer Corps (Rela) and St John Ambulance Malaysia to provide crowd management and emergency response services.
"In addition to religious activities, several community outreach programmes are being conducted, including blood donation and organ donation campaigns, the Silent Mentor programme and Green Wesak, an environmental initiative where volunteers collect recyclable waste."
Leslie said the temple hoped this year's Wesak celebration would inspire Malaysians to embrace the values of peace, compassion and goodwill.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Laos cave rescue: Diving is last resort to find remaining miners, says Malaysian diver
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Critical details provided by five rescued survivors are guiding the search for two gold miners that have been trapped inside a flooded cave in central Laos for over a week.
With conditions being hazardous, Malaysian technical cave diver Lee Kian Lie, who is part of the international rescue mission, said that diving is currently the last resort.
"Diving is the last option to search for the remaining victims for now.
"Everyone here is still highly motivated to look for the missing two based on the information given by the surviving five (miners).
"We will try our best," he told the New Straits Times.
The victims were part of a seven-member gold mining expedition in a remote village in Xaysomboun, central Laos, an area known for its limestone caves.
They were trapped in the cave on May 20, after heavy rain flooded the area and choked their exit passage.
Although the rescue team is still in good spirits to continue the mission, pumping out the floodwaters remains one of the mission's biggest challenges, Lee added.
Lee said the five who were rescued are in good health and currently in the hospital receiving treatment.
Lee, who has 13 years of experience in cave exploration was invited to join the rescue team.
Previously, Bernama reported that the search had been hampered by narrow passages, zero visibility, and worsening weather conditions.
Rescuers located five
of the seven miners on Wednesday.
One was brought out safely on May 29, while four others emerged from the cave on May 30.
Two remain missing, trapped about 300m underground.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US chemical spill death toll rises to 11
Article:
LOS ANGELES: The death toll from a chemical spill at a paper plant in Washington state has risen to 11 as rescuers recovered the bodies of all nine missing people, US authorities said on Saturday.
A huge tank holding tens of thousands of gallons of a highly caustic substance imploded on Tuesday at the plant in Longview, Washington state, sparking a major search operation.
"We have recovered the ninth and final missing employee of this incident," Brad Hannig, fire chief of Longview Fire Department, told a news conference.
Rescuers said on Wednesday that two people had died while nine others remained missing.
The accident at the Nippon Dynawave Packaging Company happened during an early morning shift change when a 3.4 million-litre tank containing a large quantity of a substance called white liquor ruptured.
White liquor is a highly alkaline solution containing sodium hydroxide and sodium sulfide that is used to break down wood chips and create the pulp from which paper is made.
Nippon Dynawave Packaging, which is a subsidiary of Japan's Nippon Paper Group, says on its website that it produces eight billion single-serve containers every year, supplying customers in North America, Asia and around the world.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indonesia faces question of structural integrity versus short-term fixes
Article:
QUESTIONS abound as the Indonesian rupiah reaches new depths. It has descended to levels last seen during the Asian financial crisis of 1997-98 against the United States dollar in recent weeks.
Even against the ringgit, the rupiah fell to a historic low of RM1 to 4,500 rupiah this week.
Ordinary Indonesians have taken to social media and some have been scathing in decrying the economic policies of President Prabowo Subianto whom they blamed for the current state of affairs.
What has brought on all this?
Prabowo has been highly vocal recently, railing against both Indonesian conglomerates as well as foreign investors for profiting unfairly in exploiting the country's natural resources, be they minerals or its vast hectarage of oil palms.
The measures that Prabowo has quite hastily announced in order to address this – principally by ordering that these commodities will henceforth be exported through a single government-linked entity – have caused alarm in the business community.
While the Indonesian leader has been careful to stress that his country welcomes investments, his very actions and public utterances probably have had the opposite effect as investors verged on near-panic and headed for the exits, exacerbating the flight from the rupiah into safe-haven currencies such as the US dollar.
Prabowo is of course right in highlighting the perennial problem of corruption and collusion of businesses in it.
Instead of being seen to be attacking this at source by rounding up corrupt officials, the blame appears to have been shifted to businesses for their collusion in corruption as well as through tax avoidance by channelling export revenues to offshore companies in jurisdictions with less onerous tax regimes.
But these are rather entrenched problems that likely defy easy and short-term drastic official actions to address them.
Good practices such as respecting Indonesian laws and encouraging businesses from skirting or outright disregarding them must necessarily be a long-term objective.
The seeming "shock-and-awe" methods favoured by Prabowo may have generated the opposite of what is intended if investors decide to stay away instead.
Prabowo may also have been motivated to take stern action because of concerns that Indonesia's budget deficit may be widening, again principally through his own action in implementing an election promise of free meals for school children across the vast archipelago.
Critics have pointed out that implementing the free-meals programme will be hugely expensive and unaffordable under Indonesia's current economic circumstances.
The hope of the Indonesian government must be that its revenues can be quickly boosted by a single agency collecting the country's export earnings.
All this perhaps understandably alarms investors and analysts alike that Indonesia is engineering a back-door nationalisation of the key pillars of its economy.
The country and especially its president have their job cut out to reassure both local and foreign players that this is not the case.
Investors and even knowledgeable ordinary Indonesians need calm, honest and transparent telling by their government that it knows exactly what it is doing and that whatever current hiccups faced are only temporary.
Public confidence is most precious given all the headwinds Indonesia is currently facing.
* The writer views developments in the nation, region and the wider world from his vantage point in Kuching
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Wesak Day draws foreign visitors impressed by cultural, spiritual experience
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The sights, sounds and traditions of Wesak Day drew not only Buddhist devotees but also visitors from abroad, many of whom described the celebration as a unique cultural and spiritual experience.
For Australian tourist Lean Mitchell, 32, who was visiting Malaysia for the first time, witnessing the annual celebration at a Buddhist temple left a lasting impression.
"I've attended cultural festivals in different countries, but this feels very peaceful and meaningful.
"The atmosphere is calm despite the large crowd, and everyone seems genuinely focused on compassion and kindness," she said.
Mitchell said she was particularly moved by the candle-lighting ceremony and the sight of devotees offering prayers and flowers.
"It gives you a chance to pause and reflect. Even as a non-Buddhist, I can appreciate the values being shared here," she said when met outside the Brickfields Buddhist Maha Vihara temple here today.
Another visitor, Kenji Tanaka, 45, from Japan, said he was fascinated by the multicultural nature of the celebration.
"In Japan, we also commemorate the Buddha's birth, but seeing people from different ethnic backgrounds gathering together here is very special. It shows how religion can unite communities," he said, sharing his experience.
Tanaka attended the celebration with his wife and daughter. He added that volunteers at the temple were welcoming and eager to explain the significance of various rituals.
Among the devotees was accountant Jackie Cheong, 38, from Setapak, who has been attending Wesak Day celebrations with his family since childhood.
"Wesak is the most important day in the Buddhist calendar. It reminds us of the Buddha's teachings on compassion, wisdom and self-discipline," he said.
Cheong said the annual observance was also an opportunity to strengthen family bonds and engage in charitable activities.
"Beyond prayers, we are encouraged to help those in need and practise kindness in our daily lives."
Retiree Sim Siew Lan, 68, from Petaling Jaya, described the celebration as a time for personal reflection and gratitude.
"Every year, I come to the temple to offer prayers and reflect on the blessings I have received. Last year, I made my offerings at the Buddhist temple in Petaling Jaya, so this year I came here.
"I normally alternate between these two temples for Wesak," she said when met.
Sim said seeing young people and visitors taking an interest in the celebration in this modern day was also encouraging.
"It shows that the message of peace and compassion remains relevant regardless of age, nationality or background."
As devotees filled temple grounds with prayers, offerings and acts of charity, Wesak Day once again highlighted the enduring appeal of the Buddha's teachings.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Foreigner killed in Odeon building fire classified as sudden death case [WATCH]
Article:
